overview.about: Thol Lake Bird Sanctuary is a shallow freshwater body bordered by marshes and agricultural land in Gujarat. Originally built as an irrigation tank in 1912, it was declared a renowned sanctuary. It hosts over 150 species of birds, making it a paradise for birdwatchers, especially during winter when migratory birds flock here.

highlights.label: 150+ Bird Species
highlights.desc: An ecological haven attracting flamingos, sarus cranes, pelicans, and waterfowl from across the globe.

highlights.label: Ramsar Wetland Site
highlights.desc: Recognized internationally as a Ramsar Wetland of Importance for preserving critical local ecology.

highlights.label: Spectacular Sunrises
highlights.desc: Mist rising off the shallow lake at dawn framed by flocks of migratory birds offers world-class photography.

highlights.label: Shallow Marsh Habitat
highlights.desc: The unique depth profile allows wading birds to hunt easily, making viewing accessible from the shoreline paths.

overview.about: The Dwarkadhish Temple, also known as the Jagat Mandir, is a Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Krishna, who is worshipped here by the name Dwarkadhish, or 'King of Dwarka'. Located in Gujarat, it is one of the destinations of the Chardham, a highly sacred Hindu pilgrimage circuit. The main shrine, supported by 72 pillars, stands 78 metres high.

highlights.label: Sacred Chardham Yatra
highlights.desc: One of the four holiest pilgrimage sites for Hindus alongside Badrinath, Puri, and Rameswaram.

highlights.label: 2,200-Year-Old Foundations
highlights.desc: Archaeological findings suggest the original temple was built by Krishna's great-grandson, Vajranabha, over 2 millennia ago.

highlights.label: 78-Metre Tall Spire
highlights.desc: The magnificent five-story main shrine, built of limestone and sand, towers over the Arabian sea coast.

highlights.label: Changing of the Flag
highlights.desc: The massive flag hoisted on the temple dome is changed five times a day in an elaborate ritual by local families.

overview.about: Matheran is Asia's only automobile-free hill station, located in the Western Ghats of Maharashtra. Known for its mild climate, well-preserved colonial architecture, and red laterite-earth roads, it offers a tranquil retreat. The famous toy train ride navigating the forested hills is a UNESCO-listed heritage marvel.

highlights.label: Automobile-Free Eco Zone
highlights.desc: No vehicles allowed inside, making it one of the most pollution-free spots in India, navigated by foot or horses.

highlights.label: 38 Panoramic Viewpoints
highlights.desc: Features numerous trails leading to sheer cliff drops like Panorama Point and Echo Point overlooking the Sahyadris.

highlights.label: Forested Walking Trails
highlights.desc: Dense covers of evergreen forests providing shade, making trekking enjoyable even during midday.

highlights.label: Heritage Toy Train
highlights.desc: The Matheran Hill Railway is a 2 ft narrow-gauge heritage railway that slowly winds up the lush forested mountains.

overview.about: The Taj Mahal ( TAHJ mə-HAHL, TAHZH -⁠; Hindustani: [t̪ɑːd͡ʒ ˈmɛɦ(ɛ)l]; lit.  'Crown of the Palace') is an ivory-white marble mausoleum on the right bank of the river Yamuna in Agra, Uttar Pradesh, India.

highlights.label: It was commissioned in...
highlights.desc: It was commissioned in 1631 by the fifth Mughal emperor, Shah Jahan (r.

highlights.label: 1628–1658) to house the...
highlights.desc: 1628–1658), to house the tomb of his beloved wife, Mumtaz Mahal; it also houses the tomb of Shah Jahan himself.

highlights.label: The tomb is the...
highlights.desc: The tomb is the centrepiece of a 17-hectare (42-acre) complex, which includes a mosque and a guest house, and is set in formal gardens bounded on three sides by a crenellated wall.

highlights.label: Construction of the mausoleum...
highlights.desc: Construction of the mausoleum was completed in 1648, but work continued on other phases of the project for another five years.

overview.about: The Gateway of India is an arch-monument, completed in 1924, on the waterfront of Mumbai, Maharashtra, India.  It was erected to commemorate the landing of King George V of the United Kingdom for his coronation as the Emperor of India in December 1911 at Strand Road near Wellington Fountain.

highlights.label: He was the first...
highlights.desc: He was the first British monarch to visit India.

highlights.label: The foundation stone was...
highlights.desc: The foundation stone was laid in March 1913 for a monument built in the Indo-Saracenic style, inspired by elements of 16th-century Gujarati architecture.

highlights.label: The final design of...
highlights.desc: The final design of the monument by architect George Wittet was sanctioned only in 1914, and construction was completed in 1924.

highlights.label: The structure is a...
highlights.desc: The structure is a memorial arch made of basalt, which is 26 metres (85 feet) high, with an architectural resemblance to a triumphial arch as well as Gujarati architecture of the time.

overview.about: Amer Fort or Amber Fort is a fort located in Amer, Rajasthan, India.  Amer is a town with an area of 4 square kilometres (1.

highlights.label: 5 sq mi) located...
highlights.desc: 5 sq mi) located 11 kilometres (6.

highlights.label: 8 mi) from Jaipur...
highlights.desc: 8 mi) from Jaipur, the capital of Rajasthan.

highlights.label: Located high on a...
highlights.desc: Located high on a hill, it is the principal tourist attraction in Jaipur.

highlights.label: Amer Fort is known...
highlights.desc: Amer Fort is known for its artistic style elements.

overview.about: The Red Fort (Lal Qila in Hindi; Hindi pronunciation: [laːl 'qɪlaː]) is a historic Mughal fort located in the Old Delhi area of Delhi, India.  It served as the main residence of the Mughal emperors.

highlights.label: Commissioned by Emperor Shah...
highlights.desc: Commissioned by Emperor Shah Jahan on the 12th of May 1639, the fort was constructed following his decision to shift the Mughal capital from Agra to Delhi.

highlights.label: Originally adorned in red...
highlights.desc: Originally adorned in red and white, the fort's design is attributed to Ustad Ahmad Lahori, the architect of the Taj Mahal.

highlights.label: The Red Fort is...
highlights.desc: The Red Fort is a prominent example of Mughal architecture from Shah Jahan's reign, combining Persian and Indian architectural styles.

highlights.label: During the invasion by...
highlights.desc: During the invasion by Nadir Shah of the Afsharid Empire in 1739, the fort was plundered and stripped of its artwork and jewels.

overview.about: The Golden Temple is a gurdwara located in Amritsar, Punjab, India.  It is the pre-eminent spiritual site of Sikhism and is one of its holiest sites, alongside the Gurdwara Darbar Sahib in Kartarpur and the Gurdwara Janam Asthan in Nankana Sahib, both in Punjab, Pakistan.

highlights.label: The sarovar (holy pool)...
highlights.desc: The sarovar (holy pool) on the site of the gurdwara was completed by the fourth Sikh Guru, Guru Ram Das, in 1577.

highlights.label: In 1604 Guru Arjan...
highlights.desc: In 1604, Guru Arjan, the fifth Sikh Guru, placed a copy of the Adi Granth in the Golden Temple and played a prominent role in its development.

highlights.label: The gurdwara was repeatedly...
highlights.desc: The gurdwara was repeatedly rebuilt by the Sikhs after they became a target of persecution and was destroyed several times by the Mughal and invading Afghan armies.

highlights.label: Maharaja Ranjit Singh after...
highlights.desc: Maharaja Ranjit Singh, after founding the Sikh Empire, rebuilt it in marble and copper in 1809, and overlaid the sanctum with gold leaf in 1830.

overview.about: Meenakshi Temple, also known as Meenakshi Sundareswarar Temple, is a historic Hindu temple located on the southern bank of the Vaigai River in Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India.  It is dedicated to Meenakshi, a form of Parvati, and her consort Sundareswarar (Shiva).

highlights.label: The temple is theologically...
highlights.desc: The temple is theologically significant as it represents a confluence of various denominations of Hinduism such as Shaivism, Shaktism, and Vaishnavism.

highlights.label: While the Sangam literature...
highlights.desc: While the Sangam literature mentions the temple city of Madurai, the existence of a temple is first referenced in the Tamil texts from 6th century CE.

highlights.label: It is one of...
highlights.desc: It is one of the Paadal Petra Sthalams, Shiva temples that are revered in the Tevaram verses composed by the Nayanars between the 6th and 11th century CE.

highlights.label: The early structures of...
highlights.desc: The early structures of the temple were built during the reign of the Pandyas in the 12th to 13th century CE.

overview.about: The Qutb Minar, also spelled Qutub Minar and Qutab Minar, is a minaret and victory tower, built during the Delhi sultanate, and comprising the Qutb complex, a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Mehrauli, South Delhi, India.  It was mostly built between 1199 and 1220, contains 399 steps, and is one of the most-frequented heritage spots in the city.

highlights.label: After defeating Prithviraj Chauhan...
highlights.desc: After defeating Prithviraj Chauhan, the last Hindu ruler of Delhi before the Ghurid conquest of the region, Qutab-ud-din Aibak initiated the construction of the victory tower, but only managed to finish the first level.

highlights.label: It was to mark...
highlights.desc: It was to mark the beginning of Islamic rule in the region.

highlights.label: Successive dynasties of the...
highlights.desc: Successive dynasties of the Delhi Sultanate continued the construction, and, in 1368, Firuz Shah Tughlaq rebuilt the top parts and added a cupola.

highlights.label: It can be compared...
highlights.desc: It can be compared to the 62-metre all-brick Minaret of Jam in Afghanistan, of c.

overview.about: The Hawa Mahal is a palace in the city of Jaipur, Rajasthan, India.  Built from red and pink sandstone, it is on the edge of the City Palace, Jaipur, and extends to the Zenana, or women's chambers.

highlights.label: Hawa Mahal also known...
highlights.desc: Hawa Mahal, also known as the “palace of winds“ was built in 1799 by  the Maharaja Sawai Pratap Singh.

highlights.label: It is considered unique...
highlights.desc: It is considered unique as it has many small windows and balconies that seem like a honeycomb.

highlights.label: The structure was built...
highlights.desc: The structure was built in 1799 by the Rajput Emperor Maharaja Sawai Pratap Singh, grandson of Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh, the founder of the city of  Jaipur of Rajasthan.

highlights.label: He was so inspired...
highlights.desc: He was so inspired by the unique structure of Khetri Mahal that he built this grand and historical palace.

overview.about: Pangong Tso or Pangong Lake (Tibetan: སྤང་གོང་མཚོ; Chinese: 班公错; pinyin: Bān gōng cuò; Hindi: पैंगोंग झील, romanized: Paiṅgoṅg jhīl) is an endorheic lake spanning eastern Ladakh and West Tibet situated at an elevation of 4,225 m (13,862 ft).  It is 134 km (83 mi) long.

highlights.label: Approximately 50% of the...
highlights.desc: Approximately 50% of the length of the overall lake lies within Tibet administered by China, 40% in Indian-administered Ladakh; the remaining 10% is disputed and is a de facto buffer zone between India and China.

highlights.label: The lake is 5...
highlights.desc: The lake is 5 km (3.

highlights.label: 1 mi) wide at...
highlights.desc: 1 mi) wide at its broadest point.

highlights.label: All together it covers...
highlights.desc: All together it covers almost 700 km2.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills
Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: The Agra Fort (Qila Agra) is a historical Mughal fort in the city of Agra, also known as  Agra's Red Fort.  Mughal emperor Humayun was crowned at this fort in 1530.

highlights.label: It was later renovated...
highlights.desc: It was later renovated by the Mughal emperor Akbar from 1565 and the present-day structure was completed in 1573.

highlights.label: It served as the...
highlights.desc: It served as the main residence of the rulers of the Mughal dynasty until 1638, when the capital was shifted from Agra to Delhi.

highlights.label: It was also known...
highlights.desc: It was also known as the "Lal-Qila" or "Qila-i-Akbari".

highlights.label: Before being captured by...
highlights.desc: Before being captured by the British, the last Indian rulers to have occupied it were the Marathas.

overview.about: Humayun's tomb (Persian: Maqbara-i Humayun) is the tomb of Mughal Emperor Humayun situated in Delhi, India.  The tomb was commissioned by Humayun's first wife and chief consort, Empress Bega Begum under her patronage in 1558, and designed by Mirak Mirza Ghiyas and his son, Sayyid Muhammad, Persian architects chosen by her.

highlights.label: It was the first...
highlights.desc: It was the first garden-tomb on the Indian subcontinent, and is located in Nizamuddin East, Delhi, close to the Dina-panah Citadel, also known as Purana Qila (Old Fort), that Humayun found in 1538.

highlights.label: It was also the...
highlights.desc: It was also the first structure to use red sandstone at such a scale.

highlights.label: The tomb was declared...
highlights.desc: The tomb was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1993, and since then has undergone extensive restoration work, which is complete.

highlights.label: Besides the main tomb...
highlights.desc: Besides the main tomb enclosure of Humayun, several smaller monuments dot the pathway leading up to it, from the main entrance in the West, including one that even pre-dates the main tomb itself, by twenty years; it is the tomb complex of Isa Khan Niazi, an Afghan noble in Sher Shah Suri's court of the Sur Empire, who fought against the Mughals, constructed in 1547 CE.

overview.about: Fatehpur Sikri (Hindi: [ˈfətɛɦpʊɾ ˈsiːkɾiː]) is a town in the Agra District of Uttar Pradesh, India.  Situated 35.

highlights.label: 7 kilometres (22...
highlights.desc: 7 kilometres (22.

highlights.label: 2 mi) from the...
highlights.desc: 2 mi) from the district headquarters of Agra, Fatehpur Sikri itself was founded as the capital of the Mughal Empire in 1571 by Emperor Akbar, serving this role from 1571 to 1585, when Akbar abandoned it due to a campaign in Punjab and was later completely abandoned in 1610.

highlights.label: The name of the...
highlights.desc: The name of the city is derived from the village called Sikri which previously occupied the location.

highlights.label: An Archaeological Survey of...
highlights.desc: An Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) excavation from 1999 to 2000 indicated that there was housing, temples and commercial centres here before Akbar built his capital.

overview.about: Mehrangarh is a historic fort located in Jodhpur, Rajasthan, India.  It stands on a hilltop, rising about 122 m (400 ft) above the surrounding plains, and the complex spans 1,200 acres (486 hectares).

highlights.label: It was initially built...
highlights.desc: It was initially built around 1459 by the Rajput ruler of Rathore clan Rao Jodha, though most of the existing structure is from the 17th century built by his successors.

highlights.label: The fort has seven...
highlights.desc: The fort has seven gates, which includes main entrance Jai Pol (meaning 'victory gate'), built by Maharaja Man Singh to commemorate his victories over the Jaipur and Bikaner armies in 1806.

highlights.label: The Fattehpol (lit...
highlights.desc: The Fattehpol (lit.

highlights.label: 'victory gate') commemorates victory...
highlights.desc: 'victory gate'), commemorates victory of Maharaja Ajit Singh over the Mughals.

overview.about: Umaid Bhawan Palace (Hindi: उम्मैद भवन पैलेस), located in Jodhpur, Rajasthan, India, is one of the world's largest private residences.  A part of the palace is managed by Taj Hotels.

highlights.label: It is named after...
highlights.desc: It is named after Maharaja Umaid Singh, grandfather of the present owner, Gaj Singh.

highlights.label: The palace has 347...
highlights.desc: The palace has 347 rooms and is the principal residence of the former Jodhpur royal family.

highlights.label: A part of the...
highlights.desc: A part of the palace is a museum.

highlights.label: The groundbreaking for the...
highlights.desc: The groundbreaking for the foundations of the building was carried out on 18 November 1929 by Maharaja Umaid Singh and the construction work was completed in 1943.

overview.about: City Palace (Raj Mahal), Udaipur is a palace complex situated in the city of Udaipur in the Indian state of Rajasthan.  It was built over a period of nearly 400 years, with contributions from several rulers of the Mewar dynasty.

highlights.label: Its construction began in...
highlights.desc: Its construction began in 1553, started by Maharana Udai Singh II of the Sisodia Rajput family as he shifted his capital from the erstwhile Chittor to the newfound city of Udaipur.

highlights.label: The palace is located...
highlights.desc: The palace is located on the east bank of Lake Pichola and has several palaces built within its complex.

highlights.label: The City Palace in...
highlights.desc: The City Palace in Udaipur was built in a flamboyant style and is considered the largest of its type in the state of Rajasthan.

highlights.label: It was built atop...
highlights.desc: It was built atop a hill, in a fusion of the Rajasthani Rajput architecture  providing a panoramic view of the city and its surroundings.

overview.about: A congregational mosque or Friday mosque (Arabic: مَسْجِد جَامِع, romanized: masjid jāmi‘, or simply جَامِع, jāmi‘; Turkish: Cami), or sometimes great mosque or grand mosque (جامع كبير, jāmi‘ kabir; Ulu Cami), is a mosque for hosting the Friday noon prayers known as jumu'ah.  It can also host the Eid prayers in situations when there is no musalla or eidgah available nearby to host the prayers.

highlights.label: In early Islamic history...
highlights.desc: In early Islamic history, the number of congregational mosques in one city was strictly limited.

highlights.label: As cities and populations...
highlights.desc: As cities and populations grew over time, it became more common for many mosques to host Friday prayers in the same area.

highlights.label: In early centuries the...
highlights.desc: In early centuries, the congregational mosque was also a center of social and public life, hosting various other activities in addition to prayers, such as judicial and educational functions.

highlights.label:
== Etymology ==
The full...
highlights.desc:
== Etymology ==
The full Arabic term for this kind of mosque is masjid jāmi‘ (مَسْجِد جَامِع), which is typically translated as "mosque of congregation" or "congregational mosque".

overview.about: India Gate, formerly known as the All India War Memorial, is a prominent war memorial situated on the eastern edge of the ceremonial axis of New Delhi India, officially known as Kartavya Path.  It stands as a memorial commemorating 74,187 soldiers of the Indian Army who died between 1914 and 1921 in the First World War, in France, Flanders, Mesopotamia, Persia, East Africa, Gallipoli and the Third Anglo-Afghan War.

highlights.label: Around 13300 servicemen’s names...
highlights.desc: Around 13,300 servicemen’s names, including those of some British soldiers and officers, are inscribed on the gate.

highlights.label: Designed by Edwin Lutyens...
highlights.desc: Designed by Edwin Lutyens, the memorial draws inspiration from ancient Roman triumphal arches such as the Arch of Constantine and is frequently compared to the Arc de Triomphe in Paris.

highlights.label: Following the Indo-Pakistani War...
highlights.desc: Following the Indo-Pakistani War of 1971, a memorial structure comprising a black marble plinth, a reversed rifle capped with a war helmet, and surrounded by four eternal flames was established beneath the archway.

highlights.label: Known as the Amar...
highlights.desc: Known as the Amar Jawan Jyoti (Flame of the Immortal Soldier), it served as India’s Tomb of the Unknown Soldier.

overview.about: The Lotus Temple is a Baháʼí House of Worship in New Delhi, India.  It was completed in December 1986.

highlights.label: Notable for its lotus-like...
highlights.desc: Notable for its lotus-like shape, it has become a prominent attraction in the city.

highlights.label: Like all Bahá’í Houses...
highlights.desc: Like all Bahá’í Houses of Worship, the Lotus Temple is open to all people, regardless of religion or any other qualification.

highlights.label: The building is composed...
highlights.desc: The building is composed of 27 free-standing marble-clad "petals" arranged in clusters of three to form nine sides, with nine doors opening onto a central hall with a height of slightly over 34 metres and a capacity of 1,300 people.

highlights.label: The Lotus Temple has...
highlights.desc: The Lotus Temple has won numerous architectural awards and has been featured in many newspaper and magazine articles.

overview.about: A Jantar Mantar (Hindustani pronunciation: [d͡ʒən̪t̪ər mən̪t̪ər]) is an assembly of stone-built astronomical instruments, designed to be used with the naked eye.  There were five Jantar Mantars in India, built between 1724 and 1735.

highlights.label: All were built at...
highlights.desc: All were built at the command of the Raja Jai Singh II, who had a keen interest in mathematics, architecture and astronomy.

highlights.label: The largest example is...
highlights.desc: The largest example is the equinoctial sundial belonging to Jaipur's assembly of instruments, consisting of a gigantic triangular gnomon with the hypotenuse parallel to the Earth's axis.

highlights.label: On either side of...
highlights.desc: On either side of the gnomon is a quadrant of a circle, parallel to the plane of the equator.

highlights.label: The instrument can be...
highlights.desc: The instrument can be used with an accuracy of about 20 seconds by a skilled observer to determine the time of day, and the declination of the Sun and the other heavenly bodies.

overview.about: Konark Sun Temple is a 13th-century CE Hindu Sun temple at Konark about 35 kilometres (22 mi) northeast from Puri city on the coastline in Puri district, Odisha, India.  The temple is attributed to king Narasingha Deva I of the Eastern Ganga dynasty about 1250 CE.

highlights.label: Dedicated to the Hindu...
highlights.desc: Dedicated to the Hindu Sun-god Surya, it reflects the pinnacle of kalingan architecture and artistic excellence, what remains of the temple complex has the appearance of a 100-foot (30 m) high chariot with immense wheels and horses, all carved from stone.

highlights.label: Once over 200 feet...
highlights.desc: Once over 200 feet (61 m) high, much of the temple is now in ruins, in particular the large shikara tower over the sanctuary; at one time this rose much higher than the mandapa that remains.

highlights.label: The structures and elements...
highlights.desc: The structures and elements that have survived are famed for their intricate artwork, iconography, and themes, including erotic kama and mithuna scenes.

highlights.label: Also called the Surya...
highlights.desc: Also called the Surya Devalaya, it is a classic illustration of the Odisha style of Architecture or Kalinga architecture.

overview.about: Mamallapuram (also known as Mahabalipuram), is a town in Chengalpattu district in the southeastern Indian state of Tamil Nadu, best known for the UNESCO World Heritage Site of 7th- and 8th-century Hindu Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram.  It is one of the famous tourist sites in India.

highlights.label: The ancient name of...
highlights.desc: The ancient name of the place is Thirukadalmallai.

highlights.label: It is a part...
highlights.desc: It is a part of Chennai Metropolitan Area.

highlights.label: It is a satellite...
highlights.desc: It is a satellite town of Chennai.

highlights.label: Mamallapuram was one of...
highlights.desc: Mamallapuram was one of two major port cities in the Pallava kingdom.

overview.about: Hampi or Hampe (Kannada: [hɐmpe]), also referred to as the Group of Monuments at Hampi, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in the town of Hampi in Vijayanagara district, east-central Karnataka, India.  Hampi predates the Vijayanagara Empire; it is mentioned in the Ramayana and the Puranas of Hinduism as Pampa Devi Tirtha Kshetra.

highlights.label: Hampi continues as a...
highlights.desc: Hampi continues as a religious centre, with the Virupaksha Temple, an active Adi Shankara-linked monastery and various monuments belonging to the old city.

highlights.label: Hampi was the capital...
highlights.desc: Hampi was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire from 1336 to 1565 (as Vijayanagara), when it was abandoned.

highlights.label: It was a fortified...
highlights.desc: It was a fortified city.

highlights.label: Chronicles left by Persian...
highlights.desc: Chronicles left by Persian and European travellers, particularly the Portuguese, say that Hampi was a prosperous, wealthy and grand city near the Tungabhadra River, with numerous temples, farms and trading markets.

overview.about: Brihadisvara Temple, called Rajarajesvaram (lit.  'Lord of Rajaraja') by its builder, and known locally as Thanjai Periya Kovil (lit.

highlights.label: 'Thanjavur Big Temple') and...
highlights.desc: 'Thanjavur Big Temple') and Peruvudaiyar Kovil (lit.

highlights.label: 'Temple of the great...
highlights.desc: 'Temple of the great lord'), is a Shaivite Hindu temple built in a Chola architectural style located on the south bank of the Cauvery river in Thanjavur, Tamil Nadu, India.

highlights.label: It is one of...
highlights.desc: It is one of the largest Hindu temples and an exemplar of Tamil architecture.

highlights.label: It is also called...
highlights.desc: It is also called Dakshina Meru (Meru of the South).

overview.about: Bodh Gayā is a religious site and place of pilgrimage associated with the Mahabodhi Temple complex, situated in the Gaya district in the Indian state of Bihar.  It is famous for being the place where Gautama Buddha is said to have attained enlightenment (Pali: bodhi) under what became known as the Bodhi Tree.

highlights.label: Since antiquity Bodh Gayā...
highlights.desc: Since antiquity, Bodh Gayā has remained the object of pilgrimage and veneration for Buddhists.

highlights.label: Archaeological finds including sculptures...
highlights.desc: Archaeological finds, including sculptures, show that the site was in use by Buddhists since the Maurya period.

highlights.label: For Buddhists Bodh Gayā...
highlights.desc: For Buddhists, Bodh Gayā is the most important of the four main pilgrimage sites related to the life of Gautama Buddha, the other three being Kushinagar, Lumbini, and Sarnath.

highlights.label: In 2002 Mahabodhi Temple...
highlights.desc: In 2002, Mahabodhi Temple, located in Bodh Gayā, became a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

overview.about: Rishikesh or Hrishikesh is a municipal corporation and tehsil of Dehradun district of the Indian state Uttarakhand.   It is situated on the right bank of the Ganges river and is a pilgrimage town for Hindus, with ancient sages and saints meditating there in search of higher knowledge.

highlights.label: There are numerous temples...
highlights.desc: There are numerous temples and ashrams built along the banks of the river.

highlights.label: The city has hosted...
highlights.desc: The city has hosted the International Yoga Festival annually on the first week of March since 1999, giving it the nickname of Yoga Capital of the World.

highlights.label: Rishikesh is a vegetarian-only...
highlights.desc: Rishikesh is a vegetarian-only and alcohol-free city.

highlights.label: Rishikesh is the starting...
highlights.desc: Rishikesh is the starting point for travelling to the four  Char Dham pilgrimage places: Badrinath, Kedarnath, Gangotri, and Yamunotri.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: Amarnath Temple is a Hindu shrine located in the Pahalgam tehsil of Anantnag district of Jammu and Kashmir, India.  It is a cave situated at an altitude of 3,888 m (12,756 ft), about 168 km from Anantnag city, the district headquarters, 141 km (88 mi) from Srinagar, the summer capital of Jammu and Kashmir, reached through either Sonamarg or Pahalgam.

highlights.label: It is an important...
highlights.desc: It is an important shrine in Hinduism.

highlights.label: The cave located in...
highlights.desc: The cave, located in Sind Valley, is surrounded by glaciers, snowy mountains and is covered with snow most of the year, except for a short period in the summer, when it is open to pilgrims.

highlights.label: In 1989 pilgrims numbered...
highlights.desc: In 1989, pilgrims numbered between 12,000 and 30,000.

highlights.label: In 2011 the numbers...
highlights.desc: In 2011, the numbers reached a peak, crossing 6.

overview.about: Vaishno Devi Temple, also known as the Shri Mata Vaishno Devi Temple and Vaishno Devi Bhavan, is a Hindu temple in Katra, Reasi district, Jammu and Kashmir, India.  Dedicated to Vaishno Devi, a manifestation of godesses Mahakali, Mahalakshmi, and Mahasarasvati, it is on Trikuta mountain at an elevation of 5,200 feet (1,500 meters).

highlights.label: The temple is 43...
highlights.desc: The temple is 43 km from the main city of Jammu and 29 km from the district headquarters Reasi town.

highlights.label: The temple is governed...
highlights.desc: The temple is governed by the Shri Mata Vaishno Devi Shrine Board (SMVDSB) and has been chaired by the Governor of Jammu and Kashmir since August 1986.

highlights.label: It is one of...
highlights.desc: It is one of the most popular Hindu pilgrimage sites in India, with millions of devotees visiting the temple annually.

highlights.label: In 2023 the board...
highlights.desc: In 2023 the board reported 9.

overview.about: The Char Dham (Hindi: चारधाम, romanized: Cārdhām transl.  the four abodes), or the Chatur Dhama (Sanskrit: चतुर्धाम, romanized: Caturdhāma), is a set of four Hindu pilgrimage sites in India, consisting of Badrinath, Dwarka, Puri and Rameswaram.

highlights.label: Badrinath Dwarka and Puri...
highlights.desc: Badrinath, Dwarka, and Puri are shrines of Vishnu, whereas Rameswaram is a shrine of Shiva.

highlights.label: Defined by Adi Shankara...
highlights.desc: Defined by Adi Shankara, each dhāma represents a particular yuga, with Badrinath representing Satya Yuga, Rameswaram representing Treta Yuga, Dwarka representing Dvapara Yuga, and Puri representing Kali Yuga.

highlights.label: Many Hindus believe that...
highlights.desc: Many Hindus believe that visiting these sites can help them achieve moksha.

highlights.label: The Char Dham is...
highlights.desc: The Char Dham is not to be confused with the Chota Char Dham which comprises Yamunotri, Gangotri, Kedarnath, Badrinath.

overview.about: The Kerala backwaters are a network of brackish lagoons and canals running parallel to the Arabian Sea along the Malabar coast of Kerala state in south-western India.  It also includes interconnected lakes, rivers, and inlets, a labyrinthine system formed by more than 900 km (560 mi) of waterways, and sometimes compared to bayous.

highlights.label: The network includes five...
highlights.desc: The network includes five large lakes linked by canals, both man made and natural, fed by 38 rivers, and extending virtually half the length of the Kerala state.

highlights.label: The backwaters were formed...
highlights.desc: The backwaters were formed by the action of waves and shore currents creating low barrier islands across the mouths of the many rivers flowing down from the Western Ghats range.

highlights.label: In the midst of...
highlights.desc: In the midst of this landscape there are a number of towns and cities, which serve as the start and end points of backwater cruises.

highlights.label: There are 34 backwaters...
highlights.desc: There are 34 backwaters in Kerala.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: The Andaman and Nicobar Islands is a union territory of India comprising 836 islands, of which only 31 are inhabited.  The islands are grouped into two main clusters: the northern Andaman Islands and the southern Nicobar Islands, separated by the 150 km (93 mi)-wide Ten Degree Channel.

highlights.label: The capital and largest...
highlights.desc: The capital and largest city of the territory, Port Blair (officially Sri Vijaya Puram), is located approximately 1,190 km (740 mi) from Chennai and 1,255 km (780 mi) from Kolkata in mainland India.

highlights.label: The islands are situated...
highlights.desc: The islands are situated between the Bay of Bengal to the west and the Andaman Sea to the east.

highlights.label: The northernmost point is...
highlights.desc: The northernmost point is 901 km (560 mi) from the mouth of the Hooghly River.

highlights.label: Indira Point located at...
highlights.desc: Indira Point, located at 6°45'10″N and 93°49'36″E on the southern tip of Great Nicobar, is the southernmost point of India.

overview.about: Pondicherry, officially known as Puducherry, is the capital and most populous city of the Union Territory of Puducherry in India.  The city is in the Puducherry district on the southeast coast of India and is surrounded by the Bay of Bengal to the east and the state of Tamil Nadu, with which it shares most of its culture, heritage, and language.

highlights.label:
== History ==

Puducherry formerly...
highlights.desc:
== History ==

Puducherry, formerly known as Pondicherry, gained its significance as "the French Riviera of the East" after the advent of French colonialisation in India.

highlights.label: Puducherry is the Tamil...
highlights.desc: Puducherry is the Tamil interpretation of "new town" and mainly derives from "Poduke", the name of the marketplace or "port town" for Roman trade in the 1st century, as mentioned in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea.

highlights.label: The settlement was once...
highlights.desc: The settlement was once an abode of learned scholars versed in the Vedas, hence it was also known as Vedapuri.

highlights.label: The history of Puducherry...
highlights.desc: The history of Puducherry can broadly be classified into two periods: pre-colonial and colonial.

overview.about: Puri Beach or the Golden beach is a beach in the city of Puri in the state of Odisha, India.  It is on the shore of the Bay of Bengal.

highlights.label: It is known for...
highlights.desc: It is known for being a tourist attraction and a Hindu sacred place.

highlights.label: The beach is the...
highlights.desc:
The beach is the site of the annual Puri Beach Festival, which is co-sponsored by the Indian Ministry of Tourism, the city of Puri
, the Development Commissioner of Handicrafts, and the Eastern Zonal Cultural Center, Kolkata.

highlights.label: The beach hosts sand...
highlights.desc: The beach hosts sand art displays, including work by international award-winning local sand artist Sudarshan Pattnaik.

highlights.label: The Golden Beach at...
highlights.desc: The Golden Beach at Puri was awarded the prestigious 'Blue Flag' tag on October 11, 2020 by the Foundation for Environment Education (FEE), Denmark.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: Shimla, also known as Simla (the official name until 1972), (Hindi: [ʃɪmla] ) is the summer capital and the largest city of the northern Indian state of Himachal Pradesh.  In 1864, Shimla was declared the summer capital of British India.

highlights.label: After independence the city...
highlights.desc: After independence, the city became the capital of East Punjab and was later made the capital city of Himachal Pradesh.

highlights.label: It is the largest...
highlights.desc: It is the largest and most developed city of Himachal Pradesh, as well as the state's principal commercial, cultural and educational centre.

highlights.label: Small hamlets were recorded...
highlights.desc: Small hamlets were recorded before 1815 when British forces took control of the area.

highlights.label: The climatic conditions attracted...
highlights.desc: The climatic conditions attracted the British to establish the city in the dense forests of the Himalayas.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: Darjeeling (, Nepali: [ˈdard͡ziliŋ], Bengali: [ˈdarˌdʒiliŋ]) is a city in the northernmost region of the Indian state of West Bengal.  Located in the Eastern Himalayas, it has an average elevation of 2,045 metres (6,709 ft).

highlights.label: To the west of...
highlights.desc: To the west of Darjeeling lies the easternmost province of Nepal, to the east the Kingdom of Bhutan, to the north the Indian state of Sikkim, and farther north the Tibet Autonomous Region of China.

highlights.label: Bangladesh lies to the...
highlights.desc: Bangladesh lies to the south and southeast, and most of the state of West Bengal lies to the south and southwest, connected to the Darjeeling region by a narrow tract.

highlights.label: Kangchenjunga the world's third-highest...
highlights.desc: Kangchenjunga, the world's third-highest mountain, rises to the north and is prominently visible on clear days.

highlights.label: In the early 19th...
highlights.desc: In the early 19th century, during East India Company rule in India, Darjeeling was identified as a potential summer retreat for British officials, soldiers, and their families.

overview.about: Spiti (pronounced as piti in Bhoti language) is a high-altitude region of the Himalayas, located in the north-eastern part of the northern Indian state of Himachal Pradesh.  The name "Spiti" means "The middle land", i.

highlights.label: The land between Tibet...
highlights.desc: the land between Tibet in China and India.

highlights.label: Spiti incorporates mainly the...
highlights.desc: Spiti incorporates mainly the valley of the Spiti River, and the valleys of several rivers that feed into the Spiti River.

highlights.label: Some of the prominent...
highlights.desc: Some of the prominent side-valleys in Spiti are the Pin valley and the Lingti valley.

highlights.desc: A remarkable attribute of this destination.

overview.about: Munnar (Malayalam pronunciation: [muːn̪ːɐːr]) is a town located in the Idukki district of the southwestern Indian state of Kerala.  Munnar is situated at around 1,600 metres (5,200 ft) above mean sea level, in the Western Ghats mountain range.

highlights.label: This ancient plantation settlement...
highlights.desc: This ancient plantation settlement, dominated by its tea industry, is also a growing modern hill station.

highlights.label:
== Etymology ==
The name...
highlights.desc:
== Etymology ==
The name Munnar is a combination of the words "Munnu" and "Aaru" in the local languages of Malayalam/Tamil meaning "three rivers", referring to its location at the confluence of the Muthirapuzha, Nallathanni and Kundali River rivers.

highlights.label:
== History ==
The region...
highlights.desc:
== History ==
The region has been inhabited by hunter-gatherer tribals like the Malayarayan and Muthuvan for thousands of years.

highlights.label: In the early days...
highlights.desc: In the early days, only Tamils and few Malayalis lived there.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: Ooty (Tamil: [uːʈ(ː)i] ; officially Udagamandalam (Tamil: [uðɐhɐmɐɳɖɐlɐm]), anglicized: Ootacamund , abbreviated as Udagai, Tamil: [uðɐhɐi]) is a town and municipality in the Nilgiris district of the Indian state of Tamil Nadu.  It is located 86 km (53 mi) northwest of Coimbatore, and is the headquarters of Nilgiris district.

highlights.label: Situated in the Nilgiri...
highlights.desc: Situated in the Nilgiri hills, it is known by the epithet "King of all the Hill Stations", and is a popular tourist destination.

highlights.label: Originally occupied by the...
highlights.desc: Originally occupied by the Toda people, the area came under the rule of the East India Company in the 18th century.

highlights.label: It later served as...
highlights.desc: It later served as the summer capital of Madras Presidency.

highlights.label: The economy is based...
highlights.desc: The economy is based on the hospitality industry serving tourism and agriculture.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: Mussoorie (Garhwali: [məsuːɾiː]) is a hill station and a municipal board in the Dehradun district of the Indian state of Uttarakhand.  It is about 26 kilometres (16 mi) from the state capital of Dehradun and 300 km (190 mi) north of the national capital of New Delhi.

highlights.label: The hill station is...
highlights.desc: The hill station is in the foothills of the Garhwal Himalayan range.

highlights.label: The adjoining town of...
highlights.desc: The adjoining town of Landour, which includes a military cantonment, is considered part of "greater Mussoorie", as are the townships Barlowganj and Jharipani.

highlights.label: Mussoorie is at an...
highlights.desc: Mussoorie is at an average altitude of 2,001 metres (6,565 ft).

highlights.label: To the northeast are...
highlights.desc: To the northeast are the Himalayan snow ranges, and to the south, the Doon Valley and Sivalik Hills ranges.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: Gangtok (Nepali: gāntok, pronounced [gantok], Sikkimese: [ɡaŋt̪ʰòk]) is the capital and the most populous city of the Indian state of Sikkim.  The seat of eponymous district, Gangtok is in the eastern Himalayan range, at an elevation of 1,650 m (5,410 ft).

highlights.label: The city's population of...
highlights.desc: The city's population of 100,000 consists of the three Sikkimese ethnicities: the Bhutias, Lepchas, Indian Gorkhas as well as plainsmen from other states of India.

highlights.label: Within the higher peaks...
highlights.desc: Within the higher peaks of the Himalayas and with a year-round mild temperate climate, Gangtok is at the centre of Sikkim's tourism industry.

highlights.label: Gangtok rose to prominence...
highlights.desc: Gangtok rose to prominence as a popular Buddhist pilgrimage site after the construction of the Enchey Monastery in 1840.

highlights.label: In 1894 the ruling...
highlights.desc: In 1894, the ruling Sikkimese Chogyal, Thutob Namgyal, transferred the capital to Gangtok.

overview.about: Ranthambore National Park is a national park in the Indian state of Rajasthan.  It covers a total area of 1,334 km2 (515 sq mi).

highlights.label: It is bounded to...
highlights.desc: It is bounded to the north by the Banas River and to the south by the Chambal River.

highlights.label: It is named after...
highlights.desc: It is named after the historic Ranthambore Fort, which lies within its boundaries in Sawai Madhopur district.

highlights.label:
== History ==
Ranthambore National...
highlights.desc:
== History ==
Ranthambore National Park was established as the Sawai Madhopur Game Sanctuary in 1955, initially covering an area of 282 km2 (109 sq mi).

highlights.label: It was declared one...
highlights.desc: It was declared one of the Project Tiger reserves in 1974.

overview.about: Jim Corbett National Park is a national park in the Nainital district of the state of Uttarakhand, India.  It was established in 1936 as the country's first national park and is named in honour of Jim Corbett, who had played a leading role in its establishment.

highlights.label: It was the first...
highlights.desc: It was the first to come under the Project Tiger initiative and encompasses an area of 520.

highlights.label: 1 sq mi) consisting...
highlights.desc: 1 sq mi) consisting of hills, riverine belts, marshy depressions, grasslands and a large lake at an elevation range of 400–1,220 m (1,300–4,000 ft).

highlights.label: It receives rainfall from...
highlights.desc: It receives rainfall from July to September.

highlights.label: Fascinating Detail
highlights.desc: A remarkable attribute of this destination.

overview.about: Kaziranga National Park is a national park in the Golaghat, Sonitpur, Biswanath and Nagaon districts of the state of Assam, India.  The park, which hosts two-thirds of the world's Indian rhinoceroses, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

highlights.label: According to a March...
highlights.desc: According to a March 2018 census conducted jointly by the Forest Department of the Government of Assam and some recognized wildlife NGOs, the rhino population in Kaziranga National Park is 2,613.

highlights.label: It comprises 1641 adult...
highlights.desc: It comprises 1,641 adult rhinos and 385 calves.

highlights.label: Kaziranga National Park was...
highlights.desc: Kaziranga National Park was declared a Tiger Reserve in 2006.

highlights.label: The park is home...
highlights.desc: The park is home to large breeding populations of Indian elephant, Wild water buffalo, and swamp deer.

overview.about: Sundarbans National Park is a national park in West Bengal, India, and core part of tiger reserve and biosphere reserve.  It is part of the Sundarbans on the Ganges Delta and adjacent to the Sundarban Reserve Forest in Bangladesh.

highlights.label: It is located to...
highlights.desc: It is located to south-west of Bangladesh.

highlights.label: The delta is densely...
highlights.desc: The delta is densely covered by mangrove forests, and is one of the largest reserves for the Bengal tiger.

highlights.label: It is also home...
highlights.desc: It is also home to a variety of bird, reptile and invertebrate species, including the salt-water crocodile.

highlights.label: The present Sundarban National...
highlights.desc: The present Sundarban National Park was declared as the core area of Sundarban Tiger Reserve in 1973 and a wildlife sanctuary in 1977.

overview.about: Periyar National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary (PNP) is a protected area located in the districts of Idukki and Pathanamthitta in Kerala, India.  It is a renowned elephant and tiger reserve.

highlights.label: The protected area encompasses...
highlights.desc: The protected area encompasses 925 km2 (357 mi2), of which 350.

highlights.label: 34 mi2) of the...
highlights.desc: 34 mi2) of the main zone was declared as the Periyar National Park in 1982.

highlights.label: The park is a...
highlights.desc: The park is a repository of rare, endemic, and endangered flora and fauna and forms the major watershed of two important rivers of Kerala: the Periyar and the Pamba.

highlights.label: Fascinating Detail
highlights.desc: A remarkable attribute of this destination.

overview.about: Bandipur National Park is a national park covering 1,456. 3 km2 (562.

highlights.label: 3 sq mi) in...
highlights.desc: 3 sq mi) in Chamarajnagar district in the Indian state of Karnataka.

highlights.label: It was established as...
highlights.desc: It was established as a tiger reserve under Project Tiger in 1973.

highlights.label: It is part of...
highlights.desc: It is part of the Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve since 1986.

highlights.label:
== History ==

The Maharaja...
highlights.desc:
== History ==

The Maharaja of the Kingdom of Mysore created a sanctuary of 90 km2 (35 sq mi) in 1931 and named it the Venugopala Wildlife Park.

overview.about: Kanha Tiger Reserve, also known as Kanha–Kisli National Park, is one of the tiger reserves of India and the largest national park of the state of Madhya Pradesh.  It covers  an area of 940 km2 (360 sq mi) in the two districts Mandla and Balaghat.

highlights.label: The park hosts Bengal...
highlights.desc: The park hosts Bengal tiger, Indian leopard, sloth bear, barasingha and dhole.

highlights.label: It is also the...
highlights.desc: It is also the first tiger reserve in India to officially introduce a mascot, Bhoorsingh the Barasingha.

highlights.label:
== Geography ==
Kanha Tiger...
highlights.desc:
== Geography ==
Kanha Tiger Reserve encompasses an area of 940 km2 (360 sq mi) in the two districts of Mandla and Balaghat in Madhya Pradesh.

highlights.label: It is divided into...
highlights.desc: It is divided into two protected areas, Hallon and Banjar, of 250 and 300 km2 (97 and 116 sq mi), respectively.

overview.about: Gir National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary, also known as Sasan Gir, is a national park and wildlife sanctuary in Gujarat, India.  It was established in 1965 in the erstwhile Nawab of Junagarh's private hunting area, with a total area of 1,410.

highlights.label: 52 sq mi) of...
highlights.desc: 52 sq mi), of which 258.

highlights.label: 89 sq mi) is...
highlights.desc: 89 sq mi) is fully protected as a national park and 1,151.

highlights.desc: A remarkable attribute of this destination.

highlights.desc: A remarkable attribute of this destination.

overview.about: Valley of Flowers National Park is an Indian national park which was established in 1982.  It is located in Chamoli in the state of Uttarakhand and is known for its meadows of endemic alpine flowers and the variety of flora.

highlights.label: This richly diverse area...
highlights.desc: This richly diverse area is also home to rare and endangered animals, including the Asiatic black bear, snow leopard, musk deer, brown bear, red fox and blue sheep.

highlights.label: Birds found in the...
highlights.desc: Birds found in the park include Himalayan monal pheasant and other high-altitude birds.

highlights.label: At 3352 to 3658...
highlights.desc: At 3,352 to 3,658 meters above sea level, the gentle landscape of the Valley of Flowers National Park complements the rugged mountain wilderness of Nanda Devi National Park to the east.

highlights.label: Together they encompass a...
highlights.desc: Together, they encompass a unique transition zone between the mountain ranges of the Zanskar and Great Himalayas.

overview.about: The Great Himalayan National Park (GHNP) is a national park in India, located in Banjar sub-division of Kullu in the state of Himachal Pradesh.  The park was established in 1984 and is spread over an area of 1171 km2; elevations within the park range between 1500 and 6000 m.

highlights.label: The Great Himalayan National...
highlights.desc: The Great Himalayan National Park is a habitat to numerous flora and more than 375 fauna species, including approximately 31 mammals, 181 birds, 3 reptiles, 9 amphibians, 11 annelids, 17 mollusks and 127 insects.

highlights.label: They are protected under...
highlights.desc: They are protected under the strict guidelines of the Wildlife Protection Act of 1972; hence any sort of hunting is not permitted.

highlights.label: In June 2014 the...
highlights.desc: In June 2014, the Great Himalayan National Park was added to the UNESCO list of World Heritage Sites, under the criterion of "outstanding significance for biodiversity conservation".

highlights.label:
== Biogeography ==
The GHNP...
highlights.desc:
== Biogeography ==
The GHNP is at the junction of world's two major biogeographic realms: the Indomalayan realm to the south and the Palearctic realm to the north.

overview.about: Dudhsagar Falls (lit.  'Sea of Milk') is a four-tiered waterfall on the Mahadayi River in the Indian state of  Goa.

highlights.label: It is 60km from...
highlights.desc:
It is 60km from Panaji by road and is located on the Belagavi–Vasco Da Gama rail route about 46km east of Madgaon and 80km south of Belagavi
.

highlights.label: Dudhsagar Falls is perched...
highlights.desc: Dudhsagar Falls is perched at the top of a crescent shaped valley and surrounded by pristine forests, and it is amongst India's tallest waterfalls with a height of 310 m (1017 feet) and an average width of 30 metres (100 feet).

highlights.label:
== Location ==
The falls...
highlights.desc:
== Location ==
The falls are located in the Bhagwan Mahaveer Sanctuary and Mollem National Park among the Western Ghats.

highlights.label: The falls are a...
highlights.desc: The falls are a punctuation mark in the journey of the Mandovi River from the Western Ghats to Panjim, where it meets the Arabian Sea.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: Athirappilly Falls (Malayalam: [ɐd̪iɾɐpːiɭːi]) is situated in Athirappilly  Panchayat in Chalakudy Taluk of Thrissur District in Kerala, India on the Chalakudy River, which originates from the upper reaches of the Western Ghats at the entrance to the Sholayar ranges.  It is the largest waterfall in Kerala, which stands tall at 81.

highlights.label: Just a short drive...
highlights.desc: Just a short drive from Athirappilly is the Vazhachal Falls, which is close to dense green forests that are home to many endangered and endemic species of flora and fauna.

highlights.label: Athirappilly Falls is the...
highlights.desc: Athirappilly Falls is the largest waterfall in Kerala and is nicknamed "The Niagara of South India".

highlights.label: Controversy about a state-proposed...
highlights.desc: Controversy about a state-proposed hydroelectric dam on the Chalakudy River above the waterfalls began in the 1990s and continued through 2017.

highlights.label: Fascinating Detail
highlights.desc: A remarkable attribute of this destination.

overview.about: Living root bridges are a kind of tree shaping in which rivers are spanned by bridges formed out of the roots of ficus plants.  Due to their being made from living, growing trees, they "show a very wide variety of structural typologies, with various aspects of particular bridges resembling characteristics of suspension bridges, cable-stayed bridges, arches, trusses, and simply-supported beams.

highlights.label: "  They are...
highlights.desc: "  They are common in the Indian state of Meghalaya.

highlights.label: The structures are handmade...
highlights.desc: The structures are handmade from the aerial roots of rubber fig trees (Ficus elastica) by the Khasi and Jaiñtia peoples of the mountainous terrain along the southern part of the Shillong Plateau.

highlights.label: Most of the bridges...
highlights.desc: Most of the bridges grow on steep slopes of subtropical moist broadleaf forest between 50 and 1,150 m (160 and 3,770 ft) above sea level.

highlights.label: As long as the...
highlights.desc: As long as the tree from which it is formed remains healthy, the roots in the bridge can naturally grow thick and strengthen.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: The Rann of Kutch (IPA: [ɾən... kət͡ːʃʰ]) is a large area of salt marshes located in the northwestern part of the Indian subcontinent, and spans the border between India and Pakistan.

highlights.label: Most of the region...
highlights.desc: Most of the region is located in the Kutch district of the Indian state of Gujarat, with a minor portion extending into the Sindh province of Pakistan.

highlights.label: It is further sub-divided...
highlights.desc: It is further sub-divided geographically into the Great Rann and Little Rann.

highlights.label: The region used to...
highlights.desc: The region used to be part of low lying peneplain submerged under the Arabian Sea, and when the waters receded, it left behind the salt marshes, which formed the Rann of Kutch.

highlights.label: The Luni River flowed...
highlights.desc: The Luni River flowed into the Rann of Kutch, however when the hydrography changed when the Rann dried up, the Luni ended up as an inland river flowing into the Thar desert to the north.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: The Thar Desert (Hindi pronunciation: [t̪ʰaːɾ]), also known as the Great Indian Desert, is an arid region in the north-western part of the Indian subcontinent stretching across India and Pakistan.  Spread across an area of 264,091 km2 (101,966 sq mi), it is one of the largest subtropical deserts in Asia by area.

highlights.label: About two-thirds of the...
highlights.desc: About two-thirds of the desert area lies in India, with the remaining in Pakistan.

highlights.label: It forms about six...
highlights.desc: It forms about six percent of the geographical area of India, with majority of the desert lying in the state of Rajasthan, with portions extending into Gujarat, Haryana, and Punjab.

highlights.label: Towards the west it...
highlights.desc: Towards the west, it extends into the provinces of Sindh and Punjab in Pakistan.

highlights.label: It is bordered by...
highlights.desc: It is bordered by the Indo-Gangetic Plain to the north, west and northeast, Rann of Kutch to the south, and the Aravali Range to the east.

overview.bestTimeSub: Monsoon for falls, Summer for hills

overview.about: The Elephanta Caves form a collection of cave temples predominantly dedicated to the Hindu god Shiva; UNESCO has designated them as a World Heritage Site.  They are located on Elephanta Island, or Gharapuri (literally meaning "the city of the caves"), in Mumbai Harbour, 10 kilometres (6.

highlights.label: 2 mi) east of...
highlights.desc: 2 mi) east of Mumbai in the Indian state of Maharashtra.

highlights.label: The Elephanta Caves were...
highlights.desc: The Elephanta Caves were primarily built by the Rashtrakuta Dynasty these rock-cut temples dedicated to Lord Shiva are considered a major achievement of Rashtrakuta art and architecture.

highlights.label: The island about 2...
highlights.desc: The island, about 2 kilometres (1.

highlights.label: 2 mi) west of...
highlights.desc: 2 mi) west of the Jawaharlal Nehru Port, features five Hindu caves, a few Buddhist stupa mounds that date back to the 2nd century BCE, and two Buddhist caves with water tanks.

overview.about: The Victoria Memorial is a large marble monument in the Maidan in Central Kolkata (Calcutta), having its entrance on the Queen's Way.  It was built between 1906 and 1921 by the British Raj.

highlights.label: It is dedicated to...
highlights.desc: It is dedicated to the memory of Queen Victoria, the Empress of India from 1876 to 1901.

highlights.label: The work of construction...
highlights.desc: The work of construction was entrusted to  Martin & Co.

highlights.label: Of Calcutta founded by...
highlights.desc: of Calcutta founded by Rajendranath Mukherjee and Thomas Acquin Martin, and work on the superstructure began in 1910.

highlights.label: It is the largest...
highlights.desc: It is the largest monument to a monarch anywhere in the world.
